@graph.name: Mercedes-Benz E-Class CDI Engine Era Map

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz E-Class E270 / E280 / E300 CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned E-Class CDI engine typically costs £1,800–£7,500 supply only depending on generation. OM612/647 5-cylinder engines sit at the lower end (£1,800–£4,500), while OM642 V6 units run £2,500–£7,500. A replacement engine can cost between £1,500 and £5,000 on average, with Mercedes engines typically at the higher end of the range. Fitting adds £800–£1,500 depending on workshop location. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-Benz E-Class E270 / E280 / E300 CDI engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which engine generation your E-Class has. The 2.7-litre inline-five (OM612/647) is highly durable — 220,000-mile examples are common, and timing chain issues are fairly rare. The 3.0-litre V6 (OM642) is smooth and powerful, but has well-documented weaknesses: oil cooler seal leaks, turbo actuator failure, NOx sensor failures that can trigger a 10-start countdown, and EGR clogging. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz E-Class E270 / E280 / E300 CDI?

acceptedAnswer.text: OM642 oil cooler seal leaks (oil loss, puddles, 'Oil Level Low' warnings), OM642 turbo actuator failure (loss of power, no boost), OM612/647 injector seal leaks ('Black Death' — carbon buildup, compression leaks), OM642 NOx sensor failure (triggers 10-start countdown), EGR clogging and swirl flap rod breakage, and intake manifold cracking. The OM642's oil cooler is deep in the V of the engine, making seal replacement a big job.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz E-Class CDI engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 70% Mercedes scrap threshold: if your E-Class is worth £4,000–£10,000 and a recon engine is £3,500–£5,000 (35–100% of the car's value), assess the overall condition carefully. Well-maintained examples with good bodywork and interior justify replacement spend. Tired examples with significant rust may not. W210 and W211 models are becoming classics — a good engine rebuild can add value if the rest of the car is in good condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz E-Class CDI engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: W210 (1999–2002) E270 CDI is OM612.961 (170 PS); W211 (2002–2005) E270 CDI is OM647.961 (177 PS); W211 (2005–2009) E280 CDI is OM642.920 (190 PS); W212 (2009–2010) E300 CDI is OM642.850 (204 PS); W212 (2010–2013) E300 CDI BlueEFFICIENCY is OM642.852 (231 PS). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the OM642 oil cooler seals, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the oil cooler seals can be replaced, though it's a very involved job. The oil cooler is located at the bottom of the middle of the engine 'V' design. Replacing the seals requires removing the intake manifold, turbocharger, and various other components. Use the updated purple Viton seals (part number 6421880580). The cost is typically £1,000–£2,500, which is significantly cheaper than a full engine replacement.

eraMap.title: The E-CLASS CDI THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: 170 PS, 370 Nm — highly durable but injector seals and low-pressure fuel pump issues
177 PS — later 5-cylinder with OM612 improvements, chain wear rare with maintenance
190 PS — smooth V6 but oil cooler seals, EGR and turbo actuator issues
204 PS — NOx sensors, swirl flap rods, and oil cooler seal leaks
231 PS — BlueEFFICIENCY, improved emissions but same core V6 vulnerabilities

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz E-Class E270 / E280 / E300 CDI Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current market rates. A replacement engine can cost between £1,500 and £5,000 on average, with Mercedes engines typically at the higher end of the range. Used OM612/OM647 engines are more readily available and generally cheaper than OM642 units. Reconditioned OM642 engines command a premium due to the complexity of the V6 design. An oil cooler seal repair on the OM642 is a very involved job, often costing £1,000–£2,000 in labour alone.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an E-Class CDI engine typically takes 10–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The OM642 V6 requires more labour than the inline-five engines due to the tighter engine bay. The oil cooler sits deep in the hot V of the OM642 engine and is a big job if the seals need replacing.

cta.label: → Compare E-Class E270 / E280 / E300 CDI engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Leak (W211/W212 V6, 2005–2013)
problems.symptoms: "Check Oil Level" or "Oil Level Low" message on the instrument cluster, decreasing oil level, oil stains or puddles when parked, oil at the bottom of the transmission bell housing. The leak often starts slowly and increases rapidly. The oil cooler sits deep in the hot V of the OM642 engine.
problems.typicalMileage: 60,000–120,000 miles — a design flaw from Mercedes with extreme temperature and vibration. Pre-2010 orange seals are the most problematic; post-2010 purple Viton seals still leak but less frequently.
problems.repairCost: £1,000–£2,500 (oil cooler seals, full seal kit, labour). Requires removing the intake manifold, turbocharger and various other components.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, oil loss can lead to engine damage.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you see oil warnings or puddles, book an inspection today.

problems.recommendation: The OM642's oil cooler seal leak is one of the most common and serious issues on this engine. The oil cooler is located deep in the V of the engine — accessing it requires removing the intake manifold, turbocharger and other components. If you notice any oil loss or see oil stains, have the seals inspected immediately. Use the updated purple Viton seals (part number 6421880580) rather than the original orange seals.

problems.title: OM642 Turbo Actuator Failure (W211/W212 V6, 2005–2013)
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, no boost condition, engine warning light, black smoke, limp mode. The variable geometry flaps inside the turbocharger fail to vary their geometry, resting in a collapsed "no boost" position.
problems.typicalMileage: 80,000–150,000 miles — the externally-mounted vane actuator fails.
problems.repairCost: £200–£500 (actuator refurbishment) / £1,500–£3,000 (complete turbocharger replacement). Mercedes will tell you to buy another turbocharger at a cost of several grand, but aftermarket actuator repair services exist.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, the vehicle will remain in limp mode.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: The OM642's turbo actuator is a common failure point. Some aftermarket turbo actuators are poorly made, but there is a cottage industry refurbishing these actuators. Have the actuator inspected before replacing the entire turbocharger.

problems.title: OM612/OM647 Injector Seal Leaks & Fuel System Issues (W210/W211 I5, 1999–2005)
problems.symptoms: "Black Death" — carbon buildup around injectors, compression leaks, rough idle, hard starting, fuel smell, black smoke, loss of power. Air bubbles in fuel lines due to hardened O-rings is a very common issue. Injector leaks can prevent the whole car from starting because rail pressure is not enough in cranking.
problems.typicalMileage: 80,000–150,000 miles — the copper injector seals fail, causing carbon buildup and compression leaks.
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,200 (injector seal replacement, injector refurbishment, low-pressure fuel pump repair).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, compression loss and poor running will worsen.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: The OM612/OM647's injector seals are a known weak point. If you notice carbon buildup around the injectors or rough running, have the seals replaced promptly. Air bubbles in the fuel lines indicate hardened O-rings — replace them before they damage the high-pressure pump. The OM612 is a robust engine, but injector issues are common.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: E-Class E270 / E280 / E300 CDI — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential repair — catch before oil loss damages engine
Actuator refurbishment is cost-effective; full turbo if damaged
Repair before "Black Death" causes compression loss
Replace failed sensors — can trigger 10-start countdown
Interference engine — piston-valve contact means little is left to repair

buyingChecks.title: Buying This E-Class CDI? Check These First
buyIf: * W210/W211 2.7 CDI (OM612/647) with full service history — the 5-cylinder engines are highly durable
* OM642 V6 with oil cooler seals already replaced with purple Viton seals
* OM642 V6 with NOx sensors replaced and no emissions warnings
* Full service history with regular oil changes (every 8,000–10,000 miles)
avoidIf: * OM642 V6 with oil leaks or "Oil Level Low" warnings — oil cooler seals are failing
* OM642 V6 with loss of power or boost — turbo actuator or turbo failure
* OM612/647 with carbon buildup around injectors — "Black Death" injector seal failure
* Any engine with timing chain rattle on startup — chain stretch is imminent

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The E-Class CDI range spans three engine families. The 2.7-litre inline-five (OM612/647) is highly durable — 220,000-mile examples are common. The 3.0-litre V6 (OM642) is smooth and powerful but has well-documented weaknesses: oil cooler seals, turbo actuators, NOx sensors and EGR systems. With the 70% Mercedes scrap threshold, a £3,500–£5,000 reconditioned engine on an E-Class valued at £4,000–£10,000 is 35–100% of the car's value — assess the overall condition carefully. W210 and W211 models are becoming classics; a good engine rebuild can add value if the rest of the car is in good condition.

cta.label: → Compare E-Class E270 / E280 / E300 CDI engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz E-Class E270 / E280 / E300 CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling on startup (timing chain wear on OM642)
* Oil Level — Check for "Oil Level Low" warnings or oil puddles (OM642 oil cooler seal leak)
* Service History — Including which engine era (OM612/647/642) and oil-interval discipline
* Injector Seals — On OM612/647, check for carbon buildup around injectors ("Black Death")
* Turbo Check — Test acceleration for power loss or boost issues (OM642 turbo actuator)
* NOx Sensors — Check for emissions warnings; failed NOx sensors can trigger 10-start countdown
* EGR System — Any hesitation or sudden drop in fuel economy? (EGR clogging on OM642)
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly.
